stories.body: Kyiv endured one of its heaviest strikes in weeks as waves of ballistic and cruise missiles plus drones tore through all 10 districts, killing at least 10 people and injuring more than 50, collapsing the first six floors of an apartment block, igniting a hotel on a central boulevard, trapping residents in multiple high-rises and forcing thousands to shelter in metro stations while Poland scrambled jets amid what Ukrainian officials called a deliberate assault on civilians even as Russia claimed it targeted military and energy sites in retaliation for Ukrainian attacks.
stories.headline: Russian missile and drone barrage devastates Kyiv

stories.body: An Amnesty International investigation based on 247 interviews, multimedia evidence, and satellite imagery concludes Sudan’s Rapid Support Forces committed murder, torture, rape, enslavement, persecution, and ethnic cleansing while besieging and capturing El Fasher, targeting non-Arab civilians, destroying towns such as Abu Zerega between late 2024 and early 2025, and leaving tens of thousands dead and hundreds of thousands displaced, prompting the group to demand a ceasefire and international protection force.
stories.headline: Amnesty alleges crimes against humanity by Sudan’s RSF in El Fasher

stories.body: Activists report at least 40 arrests, 16 imprisonments, and the shutdown of HIV services after Niger’s junta enacted a penal code criminalizing same-sex relations with up to 10 years in prison, fines reaching 100 million CFA francs, and 20-year penalties for involvement in LGBTQ+ organizations, creating what local advocates describe as a toxic climate that has driven communities underground amid broader authoritarian moves since General Abdourahamane Tchiani seized power.
stories.headline: Rights groups warn of LGBTQ+ ‘witch-hunt’ in Niger

stories.body: Following indirect technical discussions in Doha, Washington and Tehran said they would establish a communication channel by Thursday to report and document violations of their memorandum of understanding, with President Donald Trump touting denuclearization progress even as both sides acknowledged no breakthrough but described the process as moving forward despite recent hostilities.
stories.headline: US and Iran cite progress after indirect Doha talks

stories.body: More than 1,700 deaths have been confirmed and the toll could reach 10,000 after two quakes measuring 7.2 and 7.5 struck Venezuela on June 24, with survivors still being pulled from rubble, volunteers leading rescues and aid distribution in places like Catia la Mar, hospitals overwhelmed, government assistance arriving days late, and civil society filling gaps as the country already strained by political upheaval faces a mounting health crisis.
stories.headline: Venezuela reels from twin earthquakes amid slow state response

stories.body: German federal prosecutors have indicted 50-year-old Ukrainian national Serhii K, alleged to have coordinated a yacht crew that planted explosive devices on the Nord Stream 1 and 2 gas pipelines near Bornholm Island in September 2022, accusing him of attacking civilian energy infrastructure and using forged documents to charter the vessel before his 2025 arrest in Italy, while he maintains he was in Ukraine on military duty at the time.
stories.headline: Germany charges Ukrainian suspect over Nord Stream blasts

stories.body: In a 6-3 ruling along ideological lines, the Supreme Court struck down federal caps on coordinated expenditures between campaigns and party committees, with Justice Brett Kavanaugh calling the limits a severe First Amendment infringement, Republicans celebrating an immediate windfall ahead of the 2026 midterms, Democrats warning of amplified influence for billionaire donors, and reform advocates decrying another rollback of campaign finance rules following Citizens United and related decisions.
stories.headline: Supreme Court lifts limits on party–candidate coordinated spending

stories.body: The Washington Post reports that President Donald Trump routed a $500 million no-bid deal through a White House office typically responsible for repairs and furnishings—one exempt from competitive bidding rules—to construct a new ballroom, raising questions about procurement transparency as the administration bypassed normal contracting procedures.
stories.headline: White House used $500 million no-bid contract for new Trump ballroom

stories.body: Democratic socialist Melat Kiros unseated 15-term Rep. Diana DeGette in Colorado’s 1st District, Attorney General Phil Weiser toppled Sen. Michael Bennet, and other anti-establishment candidates surged as Justice Democrats, DSA organizers, and national progressive figures fueled an anti-incumbent wave that overcame heavy spending advantages and signaled potential headaches for House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries if Democrats regain a narrow majority.
stories.headline: Progressive insurgents sweep Colorado Democratic primaries

stories.body: After the government’s exceptional program to regularize undocumented residents closed with roughly 1.2 million applications—far above the initial 500,000 estimate—the National Police launched a paid “shock plan” with extended shifts, weekend hours, and accelerated fingerprinting to issue foreign identity cards, while officials defended the broad scope and provisional permits even as the Supreme Court weighs questions about EU law compliance.
stories.headline: Spain’s extraordinary migrant regularization overwhelms capacity

stories.body: With 2.4 million descendants of Spanish exiles applying for nationality under the 2022 Democratic Memory Law, the Popular Party alleges opacity, rushed procedures, and even “electoral engineering” as hundreds of thousands gain voting rights from abroad, while the government insists consular processes remain legally rigorous despite outsourcing support services and Vox demands suspending overseas mail voting altogether.
stories.headline: Government and opposition clash over ‘law of grandchildren’ naturalizations

stories.body: The Sánchez government is fast-tracking approvals for the regularization drive while preparing legal arguments with the State Attorney’s Office after the Supreme Court decided to ask the EU’s top court whether the process conflicts with European law, a move officials view as another chapter in long-running institutional clashes that also encompassed the Catalan pardons, the amnesty law, and the conviction of former attorney general Álvaro García Ortiz.
stories.headline: Supreme Court scrutiny deepens tensions over migrant policy

stories.body: The Tour de France 2026 presentation between the Hospital de Sant Pau and the Sagrada Família will draw an estimated 850,000 people and trigger extensive restrictions through Sunday, including bus detours, special shuttle lines replacing tram service, a temporary shutdown of Sagrada Família metro access, and a 14:00 lockdown of a large central perimeter until late evening as authorities urge pedestrians to dominate movement near the venue.
stories.headline: Barcelona braces for Tour de France kickoff with sweeping closures

stories.body: Businesses and households along Barcelona’s Rambla del Poblenou report daily power cuts for a week—an annual summer pattern that leaves clinics canceling appointments, residents sweltering without elevators, and people with mobility impairments stranded for hours—despite utility Endesa’s attempt to reconfigure a faulty low-voltage underground line to spread loads and prevent further outages.
stories.headline: Poblenou residents endure recurring summer blackouts

stories.body: During a parliamentary control session, President Salvador Illa urged parties to reconsider the consensus that removed tolls from the AP-7 in 2021, saying the highway has become an accident-prone gridlock and proposing to study Commons leader Jéssica Albiach’s ideas on truck restrictions while chiding lawmakers for opposing new infrastructure such as the B-40 needed to ease chronic traffic.
stories.headline: Catalan president questions ending AP-7 tolls amid congestion
